skill.name: skill-creator-agent
skill.description: Guides creation of Claude Code skills that use specialist agents, context handoff protocols, SDK examples, and integration testing patterns.
skill.summary: Create agent-backed Claude Code skills with clear handoff, lifecycle, and testing guidance.
skill.author: DNYoussef

security_audit.summary: Static high-risk hits are mostly false positives from documentation terms such as agent, spawn, prompt, and GraphViz labels. No prompt injection attempt or malicious execution logic was found, but SDK examples demonstrate broad tool permissions, web access, shell access, home-directory installation, and config-driven permissions that require user review.

medium_findings.title: Broad Tool Permissions in SDK Examples
medium_findings.description: The documentation examples configure agents with Bash, Write, WebFetch, and permissive edit modes. This is not executed by the skill, but copied implementations could allow shell commands, file writes, and network access without enough review.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The risky tools and permission modes are explicit in SDK examples. Confidence is medium because these are documentation snippets, not runnable bundled code.

medium_findings.title: User Input Passed Directly Into Agent Prompts
medium_findings.description: Examples interpolate user requests, file lists, source paths, and output formats into agent queries. Production users should validate inputs and add prompt-injection boundaries before adopting this pattern.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The interpolation points are directly visible in examples. Confidence is limited because the examples do not prove unsafe real-world data flow in this skill.

medium_findings.title: Config-Driven Agent Permissions
medium_findings.description: The configuration example loads allowed tools, permission mode, and MCP servers from a skill config. If copied without validation, an untrusted config could grant unexpected tool or network access.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The sample delegates security-sensitive options to configuration. Confidence is medium-low because the loader implementation is not present, so trust boundaries are unknown.

low_findings.title: Static Malware Keyword Findings Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The reported C2, weak cryptography, and reconnaissance hits occur in prose or diagram labels about agent architecture, prompting, resources, and SDK flow. No evidence found of command-and-control behavior, cryptographic implementation, or reconnaissance logic.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Reviewed context shows documentation and GraphViz syntax rather than executable behavior. The cited files contain no malware workflow or cryptographic code.

low_findings.title: Markdown Code Fences Misclassified as Shell Backticks
low_findings.description: The Ruby or shell backtick findings are Markdown fence boundaries and SDK examples. They do not execute inside this skill, although some examples discuss powerful tools and are covered as medium-risk guidance.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are inside or adjacent to fenced documentation examples. No standalone Ruby backtick execution pattern was found in the skill files.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded External Documentation URL
low_findings.description: The static network finding is a reference link to GraphViz guidance, not a network request. It is low risk, but users should verify external documentation before relying on it.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Line 76 is clearly prose containing an external URL. It does not trigger runtime network access in the skill.

low_findings.title: Home Directory Installation Example
low_findings.description: The installation snippet copies a sample skill into the Claude skills directory and changes into it. This is normal installation guidance, but it writes under a hidden home directory.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The filesystem access is explicit and benign installation guidance. It remains worth noting because hidden home-directory writes affect user environment state.

dangerous_patterns.title: Agent Definitions Grant Shell, Write, and Web Tools
dangerous_patterns.description: Several SDK examples include powerful tools in allowed tool lists. This pattern should be restricted to trusted repositories and reviewed before use.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The tool names are explicit in the examples. The concern is about adoption of the pattern, not direct execution by this skill.

dangerous_patterns.title: Prompt Construction Uses Direct Interpolation
dangerous_patterns.description: Example prompts include user-controlled values directly in agent requests. Implementations should delimit inputs and validate paths before execution.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: Direct interpolation is present in the snippets. The exact exploitability depends on how users adapt the documentation.

content.value_statement: Complex skills need consistent specialist behavior and clear handoff rules. This skill guides teams through designing agent-backed Claude Code skills with SDK patterns, permission planning, and integration tests.
actual_capabilities: Explains when an agent-backed skill architecture is useful.
Defines a two-layer skill and specialist agent pattern.
Provides TypeScript and Python SDK implementation examples.
Documents context handoff, result processing, and lifecycle hooks.
Recommends testing, permission isolation, and packaging practices.
limitations: It is guidance documentation, not a complete executable generator.
Example SDK code requires adaptation before production use.
It assumes users understand Claude Code skills and agent concepts.
It does not include automated validation scripts or tests.

use_cases.title: Design a Reusable Team Skill
use_cases.description: Create a skill pattern that delegates complex work to a focused specialist agent.

use_cases.title: Document Agent Handoff Rules
use_cases.description: Define what context the skill sends to the agent and how results return.

use_cases.title: Plan SDK-Based Skill Implementations
use_cases.description: Use the TypeScript and Python examples as starting points for custom agent workflows.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Basic Agent Skill
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me design a Claude Code skill that uses one specialist agent. The skill should handle [task], use [inputs], and return [output].

prompt_templates.title: Define Context Handoff
prompt_templates.prompt: Draft a context handoff protocol for an agent-backed skill. Include required fields, optional fields, validation rules, and expected result format.

prompt_templates.title: Add SDK Implementation Guidance
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a TypeScript or Python implementation plan for this agent-backed skill. Include lifecycle management, permissions, and error handling.

prompt_templates.title: Review a Multi-Agent Skill Architecture
prompt_templates.prompt: Evaluate this multi-agent skill design for security, maintainability, context boundaries, and test coverage. Recommend concrete changes before implementation.

output_examples.input: A request to create an API documentation skill with a specialist agent.
output: A skill scope that triggers on API documentation tasks.
A specialist agent role focused on endpoint analysis and documentation quality.
A handoff protocol covering source files, output format, and validation expectations.

output_examples.input: A request to improve an existing agent-backed skill design.
output: A review of the skill layer and agent layer responsibilities.
Recommendations for narrower tool permissions and clearer result validation.
A test plan for context handoff, failure handling, and final output quality.

best_practices: Keep the skill layer focused on triggering, context gathering, and result processing.
Use least-privilege tool permissions for every specialist agent.
Test context handoff and result validation with realistic examples.
anti_patterns: Do not give agents broad shell, write, or web access by default.
Do not mix detailed domain execution logic into the skill layer.
Do not pass untrusted input into agent prompts without clear boundaries.

faq.question: What does this skill help create?
faq.answer: It helps create Claude Code skills that delegate complex work to specialist agents.

faq.question: Does it generate a complete working package automatically?
faq.answer: No. It provides architecture, examples, and guidance that users adapt to their project.

faq.question: Which tools does it support?
faq.answer: The report marks it as compatible with Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.

faq.question: Why does the audit show medium risk?
faq.answer: The examples include powerful agent tools and permission modes that need review before production use.

faq.question: Is there evidence of malicious behavior?
faq.answer: No evidence found of malicious command-and-control, exfiltration, or prompt injection in the reviewed files.

faq.question: How should teams use this safely?
faq.answer: Treat SDK snippets as examples, validate inputs, restrict tools, and test permission boundaries.
